source_metadata.title_raw: Walter V. Hays - A-82-063 - April 20, 1982 -Los Gatos

content.full_text:
A-82-063

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

April 20, 1982


Walter V. Hays
Town Attorney
Town of Los Gatos
P.O. Box 949
Los Gatos, CA 95030

                                                    Re:  Your Request for Written
                                                         Advice on Behalf of Brent
                                                         Ventura
                                                    Advice Letter No. 82-063

Dear Mr. Hays:

     Thank you for your letter requesting written advice
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) on behalf of
Councilmember Brent Ventura.  As you have presented them,
the facts are as follows:

     You have incorporated the facts from a previous advice
letter regarding Councilman Ventura (Advice Letter to Preston
Hill, No. A-80-090, July 17, 1980) in which we advised that
he did have a conflict of interest regarding the initial
consideration of the rent control ordinance.  Those facts
are as follows:

     Mr. Ventura is a member of the Town Council (i.e., city
council) of the Town of Los Gatos.  He currently has a one-
sixth beneficial interest in an irrevocable testamentary
trust, and upon the death of his father, one-third (two-
sixths) of the corpus will be distributed to Mr. Ventura.
One of the two major assets of the trust is a seven-unit
apartment building in Los Gatos worth approximately $300,000
to $400,000 which grosses about $1,400 to $1,600 per month
in rents.  The present value of one-sixth of the corpus of
the trust is between $10,000 and $100,000.

     In late 1980, the Town Council did adopt a rent control
ordinance, Chapter 24 of the Town Code, with Mr. Ventura not
participating in the decision per our advice letter of 7/17/80.
Now, the Town Council has been presented with suggestions

Walter V. Hays
April 20, 1982
Page Two


for several relatively minor amendments to the ordinance.
The following additional facts apply:

     1.  Mr. Ventura still owns the same interests in the
trust referred to in our letter of 7/17/80.

     2.  Chapter 24 of the Town Code, while referred to as a
rental mediation ordinance, does in fact impose rent control.
§24.30.010 forbids a rent increase which is "unreasonable."
§24.70.015 states that any rent increase in excess of 70% of
the increase in the CPI is deemed unreasonable unless the
Hearing Officer determines that other factors render a larger
increase reasonable.  §24.70.020 lists other factors of
reasonableness, such as allowance for the pass-through of
costs of capital improvements, increased costs of maintenance,
etc.

     3.  From time to time the Council considers minor amend-
ments to the ordinance.  The enclosed examples, which are to
be considered by the Council shortly, are as follows:

     a.  Amendment to allow immediate pass-through of fees
increase.

     b.  Amendment to allow award of costs to Town.

     c.  Amendment to allow mediator to deny stay if petition
is frivolous.

     d.  Amendment to prohibit pass-through of debt service
on unsecured loans.

     You have stated your request as follows:

          Unless the property in which Mr. Ventura has an
     interest is directly affected by one of the issues
     involved in the proposed amendments, it is hard to see
     how any of them could have a significant effect on his
     interest.  Under these circumstances, he would like to
     vote if he may do so, and would like your advice on the
     subject.

     The issue is not really whether or not there is a direct
effect on his property, but rather whether the effect (if
there is one at all) is both foreseeable and material.  Upon

Walter V. Hays
April 20, 1982
Page Three


a review of the proposed amendments1/ we do not find that to
be the case as to any of them, and hence, we see no conflict
of interest arising from his participation in their considera-
tion. However, if, in the course of their consideration,
there was a significant change in any of these proposals,
then our conclusion might be altered.

Our conclusion is based upon the following analysis of
the proposed amendments.

Analysis

The Political Reform Act (hereinafter the "Act" or the
"PRA")2/ mandates that no public official may make or participate
in the making of any government decision in which he or she
knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial
interest.3/

Financial Interest

Section 87103 of the Act defines when an official has a
financial interest within the meaning of the Act. It states
as follows:

    An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

    (a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000).

    (b) Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000).

. . .


_________________________
1/ You also enclosed, as examples only, two recently
adopted amendments. Because these have already been adopted,
we offer no advice as to them.
2/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014; hereinafter
all references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.
3/ Section 87100.

Walter V. Hays
April 20, 1982
Page Four


An interest in real property, as used in the PRA, is
defined in Section 82033 as follows:

     "Interest in real property" includes any leasehold,
beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire
such an interest in real property located in the jurisdic-
tion owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the
public official, or other filer, or his or her immediate
family if the fair market value of the interest is
greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000) . . .

     It is clear, then, that Mr. Ventura has an interest in
real property worth more than $1,000 under the PRA. It may
also be true that it constitutes an investment in a business
entity. However, for purposes of our analysis, the real
property standards will be applied inasmuch as they are more
stringent.

               Foreseeable Material Financial Effect

     We must examine the reasonably foreseeable effects of
the proposed ordinance amendments upon Mr. Ventura's financial
interests.

     In the case of an interest in real property, this term
has been defined as follows:

     (a)  The financial effect of a governmental decision
on a financial interest of a public official is material
if the decision will have a significant effect on the
business entity, real property or source of income in
question.

     (b)  In determining whether it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the effects of a governmental decision
will be significant within the meaning of the general
standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration
should be given to the following factors:

                         * * *

     (2)  Whether, in the case of a direct or indirect
interest in real property of one thousand dollars ($1,000)
or more held by a public official, the effect of the
decision will be to increase or decrease:

Walter V. Hays
April 20, 1982
Page Five


     (A)  The income producing potential of the property
by the lessor of:

     1.  One thousand dollars ($1,000) per month; or

     2.  Five percent per month if it is fifty dollars ($50)
or more per month; or

     (B)  The fair market value of the property by the
lessor of:

     1.  Ten thousand dollars ($10,000); or

     2.  One half of one percent if it is one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more.

     Generally, the effect upon the value of his interest in
real property would have to be at least $1,000 to be material.

     Applying this test to the specific proposed amendments
at hand, we find:

     (a)  Amendment to allow immediate pass-through of fee
increases—This amendment could have a financial effect on
Mr. Ventura.  However, in light of the de minimus nature of
the current regulatory fee ($18.50 per year), increases,
which would be passed on more quickly, are not likely to
amount to much and, hence, any impact of the amendment would
not be material.

     (b)  Amendment to allow award of costs to Town—The
change here affects the town, not the landlord (unless he
loses).  The prospects of such a loss, on the facts given,
are so hypothetical as to not be foreseeable and they are
under the control of another—the arbitrator.

     (c)  Amendment to allow mediator to deny a stay if the
petition is frivolous—Again, foreseeability is lacking.
Any possible financial benefit to Mr. Ventura is remote and
dependent upon the actions of intervening decision-makers.

     (d)  Amendment to prohibit pass-through of debt service
on unsecured loans—This could have a material financial
effect upon Mr. Ventura if he is intending to seek such a
pass-through.  We have been given no facts to suggest that
he is in any way contemplating such a pass-through.  Conse-
quently, such an effect is not foreseeable.

Walter V. Hays
April 20, 1982
Page Six


Conclusion

     As to the specific amendments proposed, we conclude
that there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect which will occur as to Mr. Ventura's financial interest
in his seven-unit apartment building.  Consequently, he may
participate.

     Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate
to contact me at (916)322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Robert F. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:plh

Apr 12 9 07 AM BE

TOWN of LOS GATOS
Town Attorney
354-6880

April 8, 1982


State Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re:    Follow-Up on Letter to Preston Hill of 7/17/80

Dear Sir or Madam:

On July 7, 1980, Mr. Preston Hill, then Town Attorney of Los Gatos, wrote you requesting advice on behalf of Councilman Brent Ventura concerning whether his interest in a trust was sufficient to disqualify him from voting on a proposed rent control ordinance. On July 17, 1980, Sarah T. Cameron replied, giving her opinion that Mr. Ventura should not participate in decisions regarding adoption of a rent control ordinance, but suggesting that we contact you again if different issues were raised. This letter is a follow-up on the latter suggestion, and is submitted as a request for written advice under Government Code § 83114(b).

Summary of Facts and Question

In late 1980, the Town Council did adopt a rent control ordinance, Chapter 24 of the Town Code, copy enclosed, with Mr. Ventura not participating in the decision per your advice of 7/17/80. Now, the Town Council has been presented suggestions for several relatively minor amendments to the ordinance (copies also enclosed). While it seems clear that the original adoption of the ordinance could be construed as having a potential significantly significant effect on Mr. Ventura's financial interest, it appears that minor amendments to the ordinance, which do not change the basic rent ceiling, would not have such a significant effect, and would therefore not be "material." Under these circumstances, Mr. Ventura would like written advice as to whether he may participate in considering such amendments.

Material Facts

Pursuant to § 18320 of the regulations, the following material facts are provided:

1. Mr. Ventura still owns the same interests in the trust referred to in your letter of 7/17/80.

2. Chapter 24 of the Town Code, while referred to as a rental mediation ordinance, does in fact impose rent control. §24.30.010 forbids a rent increase which is "unreasonable." §24.70.015 states that any rent increase in excess of 70% of the increase in the CPI are deemed unreasonable unless the Hearing Officer

CIVIC CENTER • 110 EAST MAIN STREET • P.O. BOX 949 • LOS GATOS, CALIFORNIA 95030

Fair Political Practices Commission                -2-                April 8, 1982


determines that other factors render a larger increase reasonable.  §24.70.020 lists
other factors of reasonableness, such as allowance for the pass-through of costs of
capital improvements, increased costs of maintenance, etc.

     3.  From time to time the Council considers minor amendments to the
ordinance.  The enclosed examples, which are to be considered by the Council shortly,
are as follows:

          a.  Amendment to cover separate units on the same piece of property.

          b.  Amendment to clarify operating procedure.

          c.  Amendment to allow immediate pass-through of fee increase.

          d.  Amendment to allow award of costs to Town.

          e.  Amendment to allow mediator to deny stay if petition is frivolous.

          f.  Amendment to prohibit pass-through of debt service on unsecured loans.

     Unless the property in which Mr. Ventura has an interest is directly affected by one
of the issues involved in the proposed amendments, it is hard to see how any of them
could have a significant effect on his interest.  Under these circumstances, he would
like to vote if he may do so, and would like your advice on the subject.

     Thank you very much for your assistance in this matter.

                                                           Very truly yours,


                                                           [signature]
                                                           Walter V. Hays
                                                           Town Attorney

TOWN OF LOS GATOS the value of their property. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.10.020 Severability.

If any provision or clause of this Chapter or the
application thereof to any person is held to be invalid,
such invalidity shall not affect the other provisions or
applications of this Chapter which can be given effect
The Town Council Of The Town Of Los Gatos ORDAINS: without the invalid provision or application, and to this
SECTION 1. end, the provisions of this Chapter are declared to be
severable. (Ord. No. 1477)
Chapter 24 is added to the Los Gatos Town Code to read as
follows: 24.10.025 Termination.

Chapter 24 This ordinance will expire two years from its
Rent Disputes effective date. (Ord. No. 1477)

PART 1. GENERAL PROVISIONS PART 2. DEFINITIONS

24.10.010 Title. 24.20.010 General.

This ordinance shall be known as the Los Gatos Unless the context otherwise requires, the
Rental Dispute Mediation and Arbitration Ordinance. (Ord. No. 1477) definitions set forth in this Part govern the construction
of the Chapter. (Ord. No. 1477)
24.10.015 Declaration Of Policy And Purposes.
24.20.015 Rental Unit.
The Council finds and declares that there is a
A rental unit is any building or part of a

the Town of Los Gatos. This circumstance, coupled with

tenant as a dwelling place, except:
housing, and other factors, has put substantial upward
pressure on residential rents. (1) Rental units, located in a building

This ordinance is necessary in order to protect
-2-
the health, safety and welfare of the citizens of Los Gatos

-1-

lease.  Sublessees are tenants. (Ord. NO. 1477)

21.20.035  Housing Services.

Housing services are those services provided and
associated with the use or occupancy of a rental unit,
including but not limited to, repairs, replacement, main-
tenance, painting, light, heat, water, elevator service,
laundry facilities and privileges, janitorial service,
refuse removal, furnishings, telephone, parking, and any
other benefits, privileges or facilities. (Ord. No. 1477)

21.20.040  Rent.

Rent is the consideration, including any bonus,
benefit or gratuity, demanded or received by a landlord
for or in connection with the use or occupancy of a rental
unit, or the assignment of a lease for such a unit, including
housing services or subletting. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.20.045  Rent Increase.

A rent increase is any additional rent demanded
of or paid by a tenant for a rental unit, including any
reduction in housing services without a corresponding
reduction in the monies demanded or paid for rent. (Ord. No. 1477)

21.20.050  Voluntary Vacancy.

A voluntary vacancy occurs when a rental unit is
vacated by a tenant for reasons other than eviction. (Ord. 1477)

21.20.055  Mediator/Arbitrator Hearing Officer.

Mediator shall mean a person designated by the
Manager who is trained to conciliate rental disputes under
this Chapter.  Arbitrator shall mean the party appointed to
arbitrate such disputes under the Rules of the American

containing five or fewer dwelling units;

(2)  A room in an owner-occupied dwelling
where under the lease provisions the tenant does not have
the substantially exclusive use of a kitchen.

(3)  A single house, condominium unit or
planned development (Business and Professions Code Section
11003.0) unit.

(4)  A hotel room or similar accommodation
ordinarily let for occupancy by the same tenant for periods
of less than 15 days.

(5)  A hospital room, skilled nursing facility
or similar accommodation where the main factor in the
rental relationship is the personal care given to the
tenant.

(6)  A dwelling owned, operated or managed
by a government agency or which is exempted by State or
Federal law from rent regulation. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.20.020  Land.

Land is real property in the technical sense.
The meaning of the word includes but is not limited to
buildings, parking spaces, and mobile-home spaces. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.20.025  Lease.

A lease is an agreement -- written, oral, implied
in fact, or implied in law -- in which a landowner, for
compensation, conveys the right to possess land to someone
else for a period of time or from period to period. (Ord. No. 1477)

21.20.030  Tenant.

A tenant is a lessee, who occupies land under a

Arbitration Association.  Hearing Officer includes both

Mediators and Arbitrators. (Ord. No. 1477)

PART 3.  REGULATORY PROVISIONS

24.30.010  Reasonable Rent Increases.

No landlord shall increase the rent of any tenant

in an amount which is unreasonable.  The factors to be con-

sidered in determining whether a rent increase is reasonable

are set forth in Part 7 hereof. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.30.020  Retaliatory Evictions.

Possession of a rental unit shall not be recovered

by a landlord from a tenant who is not otherwise in default

as to the terms of occupancy of said unit if either:

     (1) The landlord's predominant reason

for seeking to recover possession of such rental unit is

retaliation against the tenant for exercising his or her

rights pursuant to this ordinance; or,

     (2) The predominant motive of the landlord

in seeking to recover possession of such rental unit is to

evade the purposes of this ordinance.

When an eviction occurs within 180 days of

the tenant's exercise or reasonable attempt to exercise

rights provided by this ordinance, if the tenant shows that

the landlord knew at the time of eviction of the tenant's

conduct in relation to this ordinance, the eviction is

presumed to be retaliatory.  This presumption applies in

judicial proceedings and is one which affects the burden

of producing evidence. (Ord. No. 1477)

-5-

PART 4.  RESOLUTION OF DISPUTES

24.30.010  Town Service.

When either party to a landlord/tenant trans-

action has a dispute with the other concerning rent,

housing services or proposed eviction, he may seek the

services of the Town by filing a written petition for

conciliation with the City Clerk; provided, that in the

case of rent disputes, 25% of the units affected must join

in the petition.  No petition may be filed later than 30

days after the rental increase takes effect; provided,

that petitions for rent increases which occurred on or

after July 1, 1980, and prior to the effective date of

this ordinance may be made within 30 days after the effective

date of this ordinance. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.40.015  Conciliation.

The Town, acting through mediators designated

by the Manager, will provide conciliation service to parties

engaged in landlord/tenant disputes.  Conciliation in this

sense is limited intervention in the dispute, using letters

and telephone and personal conversation to attempt to

explain to each party the position of the other and to urge

rational agreement. (Ord. No.1477)

24.40.020  Mediation.

When conciliation has been requested and 15

-6-

days have passed without resolution of the dispute, either
party may request mediation by applying therefor on a form
provided by the Mediator. (Ord. No. 1477)

21.40.035 Response To Mediation Requests.

When mediation has been properly requested, the
Mediator shall notify both the landlord and the tenant, in
writing, setting a date or dates for mediation conference,
specifying what documents each party is to bring and sending
each party a copy of the Rent Disputes Ordinance. (Ord. No. 1477)

21.40.040 Subpoenas.

The Mediator may, on his own initiative or at the
request of a party, issue subpoenas including subpoenas duces
tecum. For the purposes of the Rent Disputes Ordinance, the
Town Council's authority to issue subpoenas is delegated to
the Mediator, reserving to the Council full authority to
issue subpoenas for the same or other purpose. (Ord. No. 1477)

21.40.015 Conduct Of Mediation.

The parties shall cooperate with the Mediator,
stating their position on all issues, conforming with the
Mediator and each other, and providing at the Mediator's
request information and corroboration of their assertions
of fact. If the parties agree, the Mediator shall reduce
their agreement to writing and the parties shall sign it.(Ord. No. 1477).

21.40.020 Right to Arbitration.

When mediation has been properly requested and
does not result in an agreement within 20 days after the
request, either party may demand arbitration by applying

therefor on a form provided by the Mediator. An agreement
to arbitrate in the manner provided herein is deemed to
be a part of every lease. The Mediator may also inhibit
arbitration if he concludes that mediation is not feasible.(Ord. No. ]

21.40.045 Time Limit.

A demand for arbitration is ineffective unless
it is delivered to the Mediator within 35 days after the
request for mediation. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.40.050 Arbitration.

When a party properly demands arbitration, the
dispute shall be arbitrated under the Rules of the American
Arbitration Association, and judgment may be entered on
any award made. (Ord. No. 1477)

21.40.055 Costs.

If arbitration is demanded and the Arbitrator
determines that his award is not substantially more
beneficial to the party demanding arbitration than a
resolution proposed by the trained Mediator, the party
demanding arbitration shall be liable to the other party
for the latter's costs of arbitration, including attorney's
fees, not to exceed $100.00. (Ord. No. 1477)

PART 5. ENFORCEMENT

21.50.010 Retroactive Application.

This ordinance shall apply to all rent increases
effective on or after July 1, 1980, and to all landlord/
tenant disputes arising after July 1, 1980: The Council
find that this provision is necessary in order to avoid
having landlords make exorbitant rent increases

-7-                                                       -8-

pending tha final adoption of this ordinance.

(1) No landlord shall be required to refund

any rent hereunder. However, the Mediator may establish a

reasonable rent at a decreased level, which shall be ef-

fective for 12 months from the date of the Mediator's decision.
(Ord. No. 1477)

24.50.015 Stay.

Invocation by a tenant of conciliation under this

ordinance shall stay the rent increase or eviction complained

of, and any court proceedings based thereon, pending resolu-

tion of the dispute. However, the tenant shall pay whatever

rent increase is determined to be reasonable hereunder

retroactive to the date the original increase proposed by

the landlord would have been effective. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.50.020 Civil Penalties For Violation.

A finding by a Mediator of a violation of

this ordinance by a landlord shall be a complete defense to

unlawful detainer proceedings by a landlord against a tenant

not otherwise in default under his lease, and to any action

to recover rents in excess of those found reasonable.(Ord. No. 1477)

24.50.025 Non-Waiver.

Any waiver or purported waiver by a tenant of

rights granted under this Chapter prior to the time when

such rights may be exercised shall be void as contrary to

public policy. (Ord. No. 1477)

24.50.030 Standing To Enforce.

The Town of Los Gatos may enforce this ordinance

by bringing a civil action. (Ord. No. 1477)

PART 6. FEES

24.60.010 Regulatory Fee.

For the sole purpose of reimbursing the Town for the

costs of administering this ordinance, there is hereby imposed

on each rental unit, subject to the provisions of this Chapter,

a regulatory fee of $150per year which shall be paid annually

at the time at which the landlord's business license fee is due.

A landlord who fails to pay the fee within 30 days after the

Town mails its bill (or 30 days after adoption of this ordinance

as to bills sent prior thereto) shall (1) pay a 25% penalty,

plus an additional 25¢ for every additional 30 days, and shall

also not be entitled to any rent increase until the full fee and

penalties are paid. The Town Manager shall report to the Council

in time for budget hearings each year his or her recommendation

as to the amount of such fee necessary to recover such costs. (Ord. No. 14[?]
Ord. No. 14[?]

PART 7. STANDARDS OF REASONABLENESS

24.70.010 General Standards.

Mediators and Arbitrators resolving disputes under

the Los Gatos Rent Disputes Ordinance shall determine whether

rent increases are reasonable under all circumstances, taking

into consideration that the purpose of the ordinance is to

permit landlords a fair and reasonable return on the value

of their property, while at the same time protecting tenants

from arbitrary, capricious or unreasonable rent increases.

The determination of reasonableness shall be made with

reference to the following standards, unless the Mediator

or Arbitrator determines that the overall standard of

-10-

reasonableness requires other standards to be applied in
a given case.  (Ord. No. 1477)

24.70.015  Specific Standards

(1)   Initial Letting and Voluntary Vacancy

Upon the initial letting of a new unit or a voluntary
vacancy, the landlord may relet free of limits of this
ordinance, but the new tenancy shall thereafter be subject
hereto.

(2)   Frequency of Rent Increases

Rent should not ordinarily be increased more
frequently than annually, but it may be increased:

(a) When the fee imposed under Part 6 hereof
is paid, it may be passed through at the rate
of no more than $1.54 per month.

(b) When title to a rental dwelling vests in
a new landlord as a result of a change of owner-
ship.

(c) At any time, with the written consent of all
affected tenants, which shall be sought through
conciliation or mediation.

(3)   Amount of Annual Increase

Annual rent increases shall not exceed the 70% CPI
ceiling unless the Hearing Officer determines that other
factors render a larger increase reasonable.  CPI is the
Consumer Price Index -- All Urban Consumers for the San
Francisco-Oakland Area.  The most recent CPI is the bi-
monthly figure most recently available from the Bureau
of Labor Statistics.  The 70% CPI ceiling is an amount
arrived at by adjusting the current rent to reflect 70%
of the average change in the CPI since the date of the
last rent increase notice.

The formula is:

Present Rent*[Present Rent x(70% x since date of last rent)]=Rent
                     average of CPI changes      New
                                    increase notice"

as computed by the Town or its agent.

-11-

Applying the formula to a present rent of $300 and usin;
an average CPI change of +.15.12%, the following result:

300+[300 x (.7 x 15.12%)] = New Rent
300+(300 x 10.584%) = New Rent
300+31.752 = New Rent
331.752 = New Rent
$332 - New Rent
                            (Ord. 1477; Ord. 1540; 1285)

24.70.020  Other Factors of Reasonableness

(1)   Increases Deemed Reasonable.

Where the amount of the proposed rental increase

consists only of passing through one or more of the following:

(a) the fee imposed under Part 6 hereof, (b) costs of capital
improvements, (c) increased costs of maintenance and
operation, or (d) costs of rehabilitation; plus no more than
5% of the monthly rent, they shall be ordinarily deemed
reasonable, provided that:

-12-

(i) Cost figures have been established to
the reasonable satisfaction of the Hearing Officer.

(ii) The Costs of capital improvements if any
are averaged on a per unit basis and are amortized over a
period of not less than 60 months.

(iii) The costs of rehabilitation if any are
averaged on a per unit basis and are amortized over a period
not less than 36 months.

(iv) Each of the costs proposed to be passed
through to tenants, whether it be costs of capital
improvements, increased costs of operation and maintenance,
or costs of rehabilitation, bears a reasonable relationship
to the purposes for which such costs were incurred and the
value of the real property to which they are applied.

(2) When Costs Of Debt Service Deemed To Be
Reasonable.

Increased costs of debt service shall be deemed
reasonable and allowed by the Hearing Officer where the
aggregate amount of debt from whence they arise constitutes
no more than 70% of the value of the property as established
by a lender's appraisal, and no more than 80% of the cost of
such debt service is being passed through to the tenants.
Where no lender's appraisal is available, the Hearing Officer
may secure such appraisal to be paid for by the landlord as
a cost of maintenance and operation.

(3) Standards Applicable To Rent Increases Which
Exceed The Foregoing.

When the amount of any rent increase or portion

thereof exceeds any of the foregoing standards under sub-
sections 1 or 2 of this Section, the Hearing Officer shall
determine what is reasonable under the circumstances taking
into account any of the following factors on which he has
received information:

(a) In the case of increased costs of debt
service due to a sale or refinancing of the rental units
or the building or property of which the units are a part
within 12 months of the increase; (i) the arms-length nature
of the transaction, (ii) the landlord's rate of return on
the investment, (iii) the frequency of past resale or
refinances, and (iv) the extent to which prior rental
increases have made provisions for appreciation of asset
value.

(b) The rental history of the unit or the
complex of which it is a part, including: (i) the presence
or absence of past increases, (ii) the frequency of past
rent increases, (iii) the landlord's response to Proposition
13 savings, and (iv) the occupancy rate of the complex in
comparison to comparable units in the same general area.

(c) The physical condition of the rental
unit or complex of which it is a part, including the quantity
and quality of maintenance and repairs performed during
the last 12 months.

(d) Any increases or reductions of housing
services since the last rental increase before the effective
date of this ordinance.

-13-                                           -14-

(e) Other financial information which the
landlord is willing to provide.

(f) Existing market value of rents for units
similarly situated. (Ord. No. 1477)


Ordinance No. 1477 adopted October 27, 1980, Revised April 7, 1981
by Ordinance No. 1495.

                              ORDINANCE NO.    1530


     URGENCY ORDINANCE AMENDING SECTION 24.40.050 OF THE TOWN CODE TO
     ALLOW THE TOWN TO MODIFY THE RULES OF THE AMERICAN ARBITRATION
     ASSOCIATION WHERE NECESSARY FOR PRACTICAL ADMINISTRATION


     The Town Council of the Town of Los Gatos hereby ORDAINS:

                                    SECTION 1.

     Section 24.40.050 of the Town Code is hereby amended to read as follows:

          "24.40.050 Arbitration.

          When a party properly demands arbitration, the dispute shall be
     arbitrated under the rules of the American Arbitration Association, as modified
     by resolution of the Town Council."

                                    SECTION 2.

     This ordinance is for the immediate preservation of the health of the
community, in that without it, the rent mediation ordinance will be unenforceable.

ORDINANCE NO. __________

ORDINANCE OF THE TOWN OF LOS GATOS AMENDING SECTION 24.40.055
OF THE TOWN CODE TO ALLOW THE ARBITRATOR TO AWARD
COSTS TO THE TOWN AS WELL AS THE PREVAILING PARTY

The Town Council hereby ORDAINS:

SECTION 1.

Section 24.40.055 of the Town Code is hereby amended to read as follows:
"If arbitration is demanded and the Arbitrator determines that his award
is not substantially more beneficial to a party than a resolution proposed by
the Mediator, the Arbitrator may order said party to pay (1) the other party's
costs of arbitration, including attorney's fees, not to exceed $100.00, and (2)
the costs incurred by the Town in the arbitration, not to exceed $500.00."

sections.conclusion: As to the specific amendments proposed, we conclude that there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect which will occur as to Mr. Ventura's financial interest in his seven-unit apartment building. Consequently, he may participate.
sections.analysis:
The Political Reform Act (hereinafter the "Act" or the "PRA")2/ mandates that no public official may make or participate in the making of any government decision in which he or she knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest.3/

Financial Interest

Section 87103 of the Act defines when an official has a financial interest within the meaning of the Act. It states as follows:

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

...

1/ You also enclosed, as examples only, two recently adopted amendments. Because these have already been adopted, we offer no advice as to them.
; hereinafter all references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified.
3/ Section 87100.

An interest in real property, as used in the PRA, is defined in Section 82033 as follows:

"Interest in real property" includes any leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her immediate family if the fair market value of the interest is greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000) . . .

It is clear, then, that Mr. Ventura has an interest in real property worth more than $1,000 under the PRA. It may also be true that it constitutes an investment in a business entity. However, for purposes of our analysis, the real property standards will be applied inasmuch as they are more stringent.

Foreseeable Material Financial Effect

We must examine the reasonably foreseeable effects of the proposed ordinance amendments upon Mr. Ventura's financial interests.

In the case of an interest in real property, this term has been defined as follows:

(a) The financial effect of a governmental decision on a financial interest of a public official is material if the decision will have a significant effect on the business entity, real property or source of income in question.

(b) In determining whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the effects of a governmental decision will be significant within the meaning of the general standard set forth in paragraph (a), consideration should be given to the following factors:

embedding.qa_text: CONCLUSION: As to the specific amendments proposed, we conclude that there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect which will occur as to Mr. Ventura's financial interest in his seven-unit apartment building. Consequently, he may participate.
embedding.first_500_words: A-82-063 State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 April 20, 1982 Walter V. Hays Town Attorney Town of Los Gatos P.O. Box 949 Los Gatos, CA 95030 Re: Your Request for Written Advice on Behalf of Brent Ventura Advice Letter No. 82-063 Dear Mr. Hays: Thank you for your letter requesting written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) on behalf of Councilmember Brent Ventura. As you have presented them, the facts are as follows: You have incorporated the facts from a previous advice letter regarding Councilman Ventura (Advice Letter to Preston Hill, No. A-80-090, July 17, 1980) in which we advised that he did have a conflict of interest regarding the initial consideration of the rent control ordinance. Those facts are as follows: Mr. Ventura is a member of the Town Council (i.e., city council) of the Town of Los Gatos. He currently has a one- sixth beneficial interest in an irrevocable testamentary trust, and upon the death of his father, one-third (two- sixths) of the corpus will be distributed to Mr. Ventura. One of the two major assets of the trust is a seven-unit apartment building in Los Gatos worth approximately $300,000 to $400,000 which grosses about $1,400 to $1,600 per month in rents. The present value of one-sixth of the corpus of the trust is between $10,000 and $100,000. In late 1980, the Town Council did adopt a rent control ordinance, Chapter 24 of the Town Code, with Mr. Ventura not participating in the decision per our advice letter of 7/17/80. Now, the Town Council has been presented with suggestions Walter V. Hays April 20, 1982 Page Two for several relatively minor amendments to the ordinance. The following additional facts apply: 1. Mr. Ventura still owns the same interests in the trust referred to in our letter of 7/17/80. 2. Chapter 24 of the Town Code, while referred to as a rental mediation ordinance, does in fact impose rent control. §24.30.010 forbids a rent increase which is "unreasonable." §24.70.015 states that any rent increase in excess of 70% of the increase in the CPI is deemed unreasonable unless the Hearing Officer determines that other factors render a larger increase reasonable. §24.70.020 lists other factors of reasonableness, such as allowance for the pass-through of costs of capital improvements, increased costs of maintenance, etc. 3. From time to time the Council considers minor amend- ments to the ordinance. The enclosed examples, which are to be considered by the Council shortly, are as follows: a. Amendment to allow immediate pass-through of fees increase. b. Amendment to allow award of costs to Town. c. Amendment to allow mediator to deny stay if petition is frivolous. d. Amendment to prohibit pass-through of debt service on unsecured loans. You have stated your request as follows:
